text: The surgeon introduces the sieve port using an open technique during a single incision laparoscopic appendectomy.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the appendix, which is being separated from surrounding tissue using a harmonic scalpel. The bowel loop appears slightly distended, creating limited space for maneuvering. Care is taken to avoid contact between the harmonic scalpel tip and the nearby bowel as the appendix is gradually separated.

Advantage of using harmonic is that it has minimum collateral damage, no remote injury and no chances of insulation failure, direct coupling, capacitive coupling. So traction is given. This is a single incision surgery in which our azimuth angle is zero degree.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the harmonic device to cook the appendicular artery, gradually reaching and separating the base of the appendix from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses an extracorporeal Mishra's knot to secure a tubular structure, maintaining traction with an articulating grasper during the single incision laparoscopic appendectomy.

The surgeon applies an extracorporeal Mishra's knot to the base of the appendix, ensuring complete closure without leaving any stump remnants. The knot is tightened using a Vandarkar knot pusher and cut with scissors. The remaining stump is sealed with a harmonic device to prevent spillage.
The surgeon uses a harmonic device to separate the appendix from its base, ensuring the mucosa is cauterized to prevent any mucosal remnants.
The surgeon completes the appendectomy by using a grasper to hold the sealed stump of the appendix. The entire sealed stump is carefully extracted from the surgical site.
